title: 2  The validate package
text:
2.1 Essential background reading
Ideally we recommend you read Data Validation Infrastructure for R before proceeding. This paper introduces the validate package and covers the essential concepts for the rest of this training.

title: 1  Introduction
text:
Up to 50 columns and 1,000 rows
Around 10 to 100 validation rules
Many contributors who need clear, practical feedback
Tight deadlines

title: 1  Introduction
text:
1.2 Possible solutions
One option is to write some code from scratch. Such as this VBA code:
'loop through all the data with i and j as indexes for rows and columns respectively.
'Checking for missing data

For i = 8 To lastRow
    For j = 1 To lastCol
        
        'Check that the cells are not empty, if they are, add a problem.
        If Len(dataArr(i, j)) = 0 Then
            Call AddProblem(i, j, "missing_data",
            "Data from a required field is missing, please complete this cell.")
        End If       
    Next j
Next I
The main advantage of this approach is that you can get started fairly quickly.
Some disadvantages are:

The rules are difficult to find within the code and therefore maintain (same for the error messages)
A lot of code is required which introduces more risk of errors
Much of the code focuses on the mechanics of validation (i.e. looping through rows and columns) rather than the semantics (i.e. what rule are we actually validating against?)
There is little standardisation for this kind of approach (i.e. n statisticians would probably find ~n ways of implementing this check)

An alternative is to use an R package that is designed to implement validation, such as assertr and Pointblank. This example from assertr:
our.data %>%
  verify(mpg >= 0) %>%
  group_by(cyl) %>%
  summarise(avg.mpg=mean(mpg))
This seems convenient for validating data against a small number of simple rules. Especially because it’s designed to work with existing pipes.
However it has some disadvantages:

Similar to above, validation rules are difficult to find within the code
The flexibility may tempt us to spread validation across a project rather than keeping it in one place
Essentially, it’s a try catch mechanism. So it will help avoid data quality issues slipping into outputs, however it cannot provide insights into how the validation process as a whole has gone (e.g. what rules are passing/failing the most)

The Pointblank package seems similar to assertr. It has nice looking documentation and outputs (e.g. gt-based reporting). However it hasn’t yet reached version 1 on CRAN.

title: 1  Introduction
text: Such as: ‘if column A == ’some option’ then column B != 0 and column C == 0↩︎

title: 1  Introduction
text:
1.3 Our chosen solution: validate
The main way validate feels different from the other solutions is that it treats validation rules as ‘first class citizens’. This means rules can be abstracted into a dataset rather than stored as code. Such as a CSV file like this:

rules.csv







name
description
rule




is_unique_id
The id column is unique
is_unique(id)


is_complete_id
The id column is complete
is_complete(id)


headcount_plausible
The headcount is no less than full time equivalent
headcount >= fte



This approach means you can do things like:

See how many rules you have
Attach metadata to rules such as severity
Combine rulesets
Apply the same rule set to a different data without rewriting lots of code

The validate package seems like a good choice because the authors of the package have taken an academically rigorous approach and the package has been around for a while.

title: 1  Introduction
text:
1.2 Possible approaches

1.2.1 Write validation code from scratch
One option is to write custom code to flag issues like missing data. Here is an example in VBA:
'loop through all the data with i and j as indexes for rows and columns respectively.
'Checking for missing data

For i = 8 To lastRow
    For j = 1 To lastCol
        
        'Check that the cells are not empty, if they are, add a problem.
        If Len(dataArr(i, j)) = 0 Then
            Call AddProblem(i, j, "missing_data",
            "Data from a required field is missing, please complete this cell.")
        End If       
    Next j
Next I
Pros

Quick to start

Cons

Rules and error messages are buried in code and hard to maintain
Requires a lot of code, which increases the risk of mistakes
Focuses on how to check data, not what the rules are
Little consistency between different analysts’ solutions



1.2.2 Incorporate validation into pipes with assertr or pointblank
These packages let you add checks directly into your R pipelines like this:
our.data %>%
  verify(mpg >= 0) %>%
  group_by(cyl) %>%
  summarise(avg.mpg=mean(mpg))
Pros

Convenient for small numbers of simple rules
Can work well with pipes

Cons

Validation rules are still mixed in with other code
Checks can end up scattered across a project
Mainly stop bad data flowing through, but don’t summarise how validation performed overall

Pointblank is similar, but there is not yet a stable (v1) CRAN release.

title: 1  Introduction
text:
1.3 Our chosen approach: validate
The validate package treats validation rules as data, not code. Rules can be stored in a file (like a CSV), separate from the analysis, for example:

rules.csv







name
description
rule




is_unique_id
The id column is unique
is_unique(id)


is_complete_id
The id column is complete
is_complete(id)


headcount_plausible
The headcount is no less than full time equivalent
headcount >= fte



Some of the benefits of storing rules as data are:

Manipulate rule sets easily (e.g. create, read, update, delete)
Manage rule sets easily (e.g. count, add metadata, reuse or combine rule sets)
Apply the same rules to multiple datasets without rewriting code
Track rule changes over time
Update rules without changing the code
Easier to explain and agree rules with stakeholders
Less custom, repetitive code
Scales well for large or complex rule sets
Supports any type of rule
Use validatetools to spot redundancies and problematic rules

Some of the drawbacks to this approach are:

It takes time to learn (hence this training document)
Producing clear validation outputs takes additional effort (explaining what’s wrong, where, and which rules failed)
Easy mistake: forgetting to add = TRUE to functions that return TRUE/FALSE values

title: 2  The validate package
text:
2.2 An introductory example 🐕
First we load some packages.

# 1. Load the packages ########################################################
library(readxl)
library(validate)
library(yaml)
library(dplyr)
library(gt)
library(jsonlite)

We then read in the data and take a look.

# 2. Load the data, validation rules, and code list ###########################
## 2.1 Load the data ----------------------------------------------------------
dog_walks <- readxl::read_excel(
  path = "data/dog_walks.xlsx",
  sheet = "dog_walks")

gt(dog_walks)

We have a data set of dog walks; including each dog’s name and how many squirrels they spotted during their walk.
The rules for this data set are stored in a CSV file. We read these into R using the validate::validator function. This creates a validator object.

## 2.2 Load the validation rules -----------------------------------------------
rules <- validate::validator(.data = read.csv(file = "data/dog_walks_rules.csv"))

## 2.3 View the validation rules -----------------------------------------------
gt(as.data.frame(rules))







name
label
description
origin
created
language
severity
rule




column_names_as_expected

Column names are as expected.

2026-05-07 10:49:58.48802
validate 1.1.7
error
names(.) == c("walk_id", "dog_name", "dog_breed", "walker", "date_of_walk", "distance_km", "duration_min", "squirrels_spotted")


expected_data_type_character

Character columns are of type character (i.e. text).

2026-05-07 10:49:58.48802
validate 1.1.7
error
is.character(dog_name)


is_unique_id

This column has unique values.

2026-05-07 10:49:58.48802
validate 1.1.7
error
is_unique(walk_id)


is_complete_id

The id column is complete.

2026-05-07 10:49:58.48802
validate 1.1.7
error
is_complete(walk_id)


realistic_distance

All distances are positive

2026-05-07 10:49:58.48802
validate 1.1.7

distance_km - 0 >= -1e-08


realistic_duration

All durations are positive

2026-05-07 10:49:58.48802

duration_min - 0 >= -1e-08


realistic_squirrels

All durations are positive

2026-05-07 10:49:58.48802
validate 1.1.7
error
in_range(squirrels_spotted, min = 0, max = 10)


realistic_speed

All dog walks average no more than 15 kilometres per hour.

2026-05-07 10:49:58.48802
validate 1.1.7
error
distance_km/duration_min * 60 < 15


distance_rex

Rex always walks between 3 and 5 kilometres.

2026-05-07 10:49:58.48802
validate 1.1.7
error
dog_name != "Rex" | (in_range(distance_km, min = 3, max = 5))


expected_walker

The walker column only contains expected values.

2026-05-07 10:49:58.48802
validate 1.1.7
error
walker %vin% code_list[["walker"]]







You may notice that some rules refer to code_list. These are the valid options for certain columns in the data set. These are defined in a YAML file.

## 2.4 Load the code list ------------------------------------------------------
code_list <- yaml::yaml.load_file(input = "data/dog_walks_code_list.yml")

## 2.5 View the code list ------------------------------------------------------
jsonlite::toJSON(code_list, pretty = TRUE)

{
  "dog_breed": ["Labrador", "Border Collie", "Poodle", "Beagle", "Mixed", "Unknown"],
  "walker": ["owner", "dog walker", "family member", "unknown"]
} 


Now we can validate the data by creating a validation object.
If you find it helpful, you can think of the validator object as an investigator and the validation object as the investigator’s report on the dataset it’s validating.
The summary function creates a summary of the validation with one row per rule.

# 3. Confront the data with the validation rules ##############################
validation <- validate::confront(
  dat = dog_walks
  , x = rules
  , key = "walk_id")

## 3.1 View a summary of the validation ---------------------------------------
validation %>% 
  summary() %>% 
  gt()

  as.data.frame() %>% 
  filter(value == FALSE) %>%
  gt()

Validation is now complete, but at this point only R users can see the results.
The next step is to turn these results into clear, practical feedback to help data providers resolve any issues.
